finding_text: Reported events involving private military and security companies grew steadily from 1990 to 2012 across all three regions, consistent with the widely held view that the private security industry has expanded since the end of the Cold War.
method_used: Aggregate event counts by year of occurrence (Figure 1)
model_specification: Figure 1: aggregate event counts plotted by year, 1990–2012
scope_conditions: Africa, Latin America, Southeast Asia, 1990–2012; English-language international and regional news sources only.

finding_text: The most frequently reported PMSC event type is routine work, followed by crime events; violent events are a smaller share overall though substantial in Africa and Southeast Asia.
method_used: Cross-tabulation of event count by event type and region (Figure 2)

finding_text: PMSC clients are dominated by national governments (about 22% of events) and local commercial actors (about 26%); transnational commercial clients and rebel groups (about 9%) are less common, with substantial regional variation in client mix.
method_used: Cross-tabulation of event count by client type and region
model_specification: Descriptive shares reported in narrative text and figures
scope_conditions: Africa, Latin America, Southeast Asia, 1990–2012; regional patterns: Africa → governments + local commercial + rebels; Latin America → local commercial dominant; Southeast Asia → local commercial roughly equal to transnational commercial.

finding_text: Site security is by far the most common service performed in PMSC events, followed by operational support; advice and training, intelligence, and logistical support are rarely captured, likely reflecting media-attention bias toward visible armed activity.
method_used: Cross-tabulation of event count by service type and region
scope_conditions: Africa, Latin America, Southeast Asia, 1990–2012; confidence is moderate because non-visible services (intelligence, training) are systematically undercounted by news sources.

finding_text: Most reported PMSC events do not involve human-rights abuse allegations, but the share of events with allegations has risen unevenly over time and varies sharply by client type — national-government and local-commercial clients are most associated with allegations, transnational-commercial clients least.
method_used: Cross-tabulation of allegation indicator by client type, region, and year (Figures 3, 4, 5)
scope_conditions: Africa, Latin America, Southeast Asia, 1990–2012; reporting allegations is not the same as substantiation, and later reporting may surface allegations for past events.

finding_text: Reports of PMSCs hired by governments and involved in events are associated with shorter civil wars: each one-unit increase in the count of government-hired PMSC events raises the hazard of war termination by approximately 30%.
effect_size: Hazard ratio = 1.299 (Model 2); 1.307 (Model 3) — exponentiated coefficient on Events PMCs (government)
model_specification: Cox proportional-hazards on civil war duration in Africa 1990–2008; Table 2, Model 2. Controls: GDP per capita (logged), ethnic fractionalization, ethnic wars, intensity, mountainous terrain, polity, proportion of forces, support rebels, support government.
method_used: Cox proportional-hazards survival model on conflict-year panel
scope_conditions: African civil wars 1990–2008 per Correlates of War v4.0 coding; replication panel from Akcinaroglu & Radziszewski (2013).

finding_text: When the count of government-hired PMSC events is included alongside Akcinaroglu & Radziszewski's PMSC competition variable, competition loses statistical significance while events remain significant — challenging the claim that market competition among PMSCs is the operative mechanism shortening conflicts.
effect_size: Competition PMCs (government) hazard ratio = 1.086 (SE 0.172, n.s.) in Model 3 vs. 1.294 (SE 0.151, p<0.05) in Model 1
model_specification: Cox proportional-hazards Model 3: A&R's full specification + PSED government and rebel event counts. Table 2, Model 3.
method_used: Cox proportional-hazards survival model with collinearity check (correlation of competition and event-count coefficients = -0.6017)
scope_conditions: African civil wars 1990–2008. Authors note the result does not rule out a role for competition but suggests event participation captures a distinct (and stronger) signal — possibly proxying government resource commitment.
